segments.text: Te Tiriti and a second-term city councillor for
Hamilton City Council. So thank you for the
time today. I appreciate it.
We've just
had some information presented to us at council around
our perceptions of alcohol-related harm in the
community,
which was a survey we did
earlier in the year,
which had about 450 submissions. And in
that, the community clearly communicated to us
a concern for the levels of harm that are still
evident in our community around alcohol.
So it's with that in mind
that I bring my submission to you today, thinking
that our community is still very concerned with the level of harm that they're
seeing,
and in particular, the levels of
crime and violence that are associated with alcohol in
our community. So a concern overall for general safety.
So in making my submission, I guess my main
concern is that the amendment proposed
appears to
increase alcohol availability in our community.
And we know that increased
alcohol availability is associated with increased harm.
I think that's been well-researched and
understood. And so my concern with
the proposals is that it basically looks to
I guess not recognize the harm that's associated with
alcohol still, and
I don't believe that it's in our community's best interest to increase
that availability no matter how it might be
derived.
Anna, can-
So-
Anna,
we're going to run out of time, and-
Yes
... I'm keen to ask a question.
Okay.
Because you're the first councillor we've had.
Tell me-
Oh, is that right? Okay.
Well, we may have had a couple, but not many.
So one of the big issues is a lot of this is administered
by councils. So even in your Hamilton, you're a second-term
councillor, even in your Hamilton area-
Mm-hmm
... what rules do you have in your licensing which says
that you cannot have
an off license
within 200 meters of each other? Or do you have restrictions like that
in your laws?
Or do you-
No, we don't. We don't have a local alcohol policy in Hamilton, so
that's part of the conversation that we're having at the moment.
So
my understanding is our licensing is largely directed by the act.
So,
yeah. So we are looking-
So that's part of the problem as well.
Yeah. It is part of the problem. And I guess that's one of the
concerns that I have with the amendment, and I do need some clarification on it,
because my understanding is you're looking to restrict the objectors to
if they live within one kilometer of an off license.
But I heard a submission earlier today where you were looking to
clarify that. So is that not the case?
Because that was my understanding, that you're looking to restrict
objections to within 1K of an outlet.
No, that's not right. It's within-
Okay
... the area, licensing area.
Because that would be really concerning, yeah, for me, because I think that
harm extends across the city and people are concerned
on a general level and should be able to object
around particular licenses in particular areas.
Yeah. Of course.
Hey, tell me, your survey, is that
something you can make available through the appropriate channels to the committee?
Oh, yeah, definitely. Yeah.
It signals, I guess, that there was some
suggestion that perhaps the numbers of bars and taverns, et cetera, that we've got
is about right. There was a concern,
though, a quite high percent, I think over 60% of
people were concerned that we had too many bottle stores.
So
in Hamilton we do have,
because we're quite a compact city, so we have a higher number of bottle
stores
in an area sense than a lot of other
locations. So we're, I would say, quite saturated in that way
with the provision of alcohol, and we just don't need more.
So that was perhaps my main point was,
and I understand people might want to go and have a haircut and
get a glass of wine, but actually, we've got alcohol
everywhere and perhaps there's some spaces that we can just leave
and reduce the availability, recognizing the harm that
exists
already. So yeah, that was-
All right
... the main point that I wanted to make.
And
local alcohol policies, I think are really important, and we're hoping to
get one across-
Okay
... the line.
All right. Hey-
And so I don't want the
standing of those reduced in any way, which I understand is part of the amendment
as well.
All right. Thank you very much, Councillor.
Yeah.
Very good.
All right.
Thank you very much. All right.
We're going to Dr. Rose Crossan.
Hopefully she's available.
Kia ora. I am available.
I'm just trying to get my video to work for you.
Well, we can hear you clearly, and we can see you wonderfully now.
Now you can see me.
Right. We've got five minutes with you, so we haven't got long.
Sure. Kia ora. So my name is Dr. Rose Crossan.
I'm a senior lecturer in the Department of Public Health in the Faculty of Medicine
at the University of Otago Christchurch.
I have a PhD in addiction studies, and my specialist area of research
expertise is drug and alcohol harm.
And I put in a submission on behalf of a group of academics from our Faculty
of Medicine at the University of Otago who have diverse expertise, as
you would've seen.We do not support the
amendments in the Sale and Supply of Alcohol Act Amendment Bill,
because we believe it'll increase access to alcohol and lessens the
ability for communities to have a say in the amount of alcohol in their
neighborhoods. And as well as being contrary to evidence of what
works to reduce alcohol-related harm, we're particularly concerned about the
impacts on other parts of the health and social system.
For example, the government has a goal to reduce stay times in emergency
departments, yet alcohol-related presentations contribute to
ED wait times and increase overcrowding.
The government's recently released a mental health and well-being strategy, which
is aimed at reducing mental health issues in New Zealand,
yet alcohol consumption is associated with depression and anxiety.
And the government's released an updated suicide prevention action plan aimed at
reducing suicide, yet alcohol is associated with increased
suicidal behavior and action. So we believe that the Sale and
Supply of Alcohol Act needs to be re-weighted back towards Objective
B, so that the diverse social, economic, and health
harms of alcohol are minimized.
Right.
I'd be very happy to take questions.
Right. Members, questions from any?
Hey, maybe just while people are thinking, I was just asking this
question. One of the big issues is at a local level,
central government always sets the overarching framework.
But at a local level, councils and district licensing
authorities have the ability to say no, or maybe
they don't have sufficient rules around how many
off licenses can be located. Have you got any
information on that? In terms of-- Because that's where the rubber hits the
road, isn't it? Because whether you allow 200
meters next door to the next one, and you allow them all four times around
the same intersection. Have you got any insight into that?
So the research evidence is very
strongly associated between the increasing alcohol
outlet density and increased harms, whether that be
crime or other issues. So there is clearly a
relationship in the evidence between alcohol outlet density
and the harms that are seen.
As I understand it, but please correct me if I'm wrong, these
amendments would mean that even if a council
had a local alcohol policy and was trying to reduce the
number of alcohol outlets in the area, that they would be
unable to refuse to renew a license that was then
inconsistent with a new LAP. Is that-- So,
in which case then, it is taking away the power of an
LAP to reflect the
community's desires and
the evidence of harm.
Okay. Well, that's going to be a question we ask.
All right. Members, any other questions from anyone?
All right. Hey, fantastic. Hey, thank you, Dr. Crossan.
That's very good that you've come in on this afternoon.
Thank you for having me.
Right. Okay. Now, we've got the New Zealand Winegrowers Association, Philip
Gregan and Sarah Wilson.
You have.
How are you?
Very good.
Right. Rip into it.
Very good. Thank you, Mr. Chair.
We've got 10 minutes with you.
Thank you. Philip Gregan, CEO of New Zealand Winegrowers, and
Sarah Wilson,
General Manager Advocacy at New Zealand Winegrowers.
I'll just make a couple of
introductory comments,
and then I'll speak about one particular aspect of the bill, and then
Sarah will just address a couple of others.
In general sense, we're supportive
of the
legislation. By way of background, we have
around about 240 winery cellar doors
throughout New Zealand. Winery cellar doors are generally
located at the winery or on a vineyard, which is the
source of the grapes or the wine. So their
location is intrinsically tied to
the origin of the grapes and the wine, and that's an
important thing for us within considering the
context of this bill.
Wineries and our members obviously invest very heavily
in
their facilities, in their winery
cellar doors, and that investment is
made with a view to the long term,
obviously.
Look, the two sort of particular aspects that I would just like to
address before I hand over to Sarah is,
first,
around the issue of objections to applications and
the formal right to respond to
objections. We support these changes.
We believe that
the investment in winery cellar door and vineyards, these things
are important investments for
businesses.
And I think being able to respond to objections is important.
In terms of the out-of-region objector, we
see the overarching
national law setting the framework in which regional
decisions are made. As a national organization, we do not
get involved in
individual license applications.
We see our role as being involved in, as we're doing at the
moment, submitting on the legislation.
And we think the restriction to the local area
makes
practical sense.The second point I'd
like to make is around district licensing committees and
LAPS. As I said, our cellar doors tend to
be located on the vineyards.
They
represent a significant investment,
and
if the government, for example, Ministry of Education, decided to
locate a school within 200 or 300 meters
of one of our cellar doors,
that might be in contradiction to an
LAP, and we do not see that as a reason
why
the
license should not be renewed
simply because of that
change. And within that context, winery cellar doors
are in the lowest risk category
of licenses.
So I'll just rest there and just hand over to Sarah.
Thank you. I'll go into a little more detail on two of the points raised in our
submission. The first is around the definition of winery cellar door.
That definition was adopted in 2024 as part of the Winery Cellar Door
Tasting Amendment Bill,
and our request is that the definition is retained and utilized
in Section 48 of the legislation. So that definition was
specifically developed to ensure that the different cellar door business
models could be captured.
The definition in Section 48 is more restrictive and excludes some
businesses that would be potentially thought of as a winery cellar
door. So for example, in Section 48, there's a reference
to produce harvested from land on which the premises are
situated. So you're thinking about a cellar door where the vineyard you can see
out the window is there, and that's where the wine is made from.
But the wording in the Section 5
definition refers to land occupied by the license holder for the
premises. So that doesn't just allow for vineyard A outside the window, it might
allow for vineyards B, C, and D, which could be up the road, it could be
elsewhere in the region, or it could even be in another region entirely.
So for that reason, we'd ask if the Section 48 definition could
be repurposed for a winery cellar door.
The second point that I'll briefly raise is around Section 52 and
the ability to use zero alcohol beer, wine, and
spirits for the purposes of meeting the criteria to
provide what was previously low-alcohol products.
While we are seeing significant growth of lighter wines and zero-alcohol
wines, we're not currently aware of anyone producing a wine that's between
1.5% and 2.5% alcohol in that specific range.
So we welcome this additional flexibility to allow a 0% wine to meet
that purpose of the legislation. We've also raised a second
point, which is around Section 52, subsection 2,
which creates an exemption from the
requirements if a business is only selling beverages that they've produced
themselves. The comment that we make is around the need for that to
be noted on the license as a specific exemption, and we would
suggest that it should be readily apparent whether a business falls into that
category that's anticipated by Section 52 [2] , and
therefore that the need for an additional license exemption should be removed.
Happy to take any questions. It looks like we might have time.
Okay. We've got Honorable Dr. Duncan Webb.
Thank you, Mr. Chair, and thank you for that submission.
My question is around the definition questions.
When the
Tasting Amendment Bill went through, one of the
fraught questions was whether that could be extended to
on-site brew tasting provisions could be
extended to on-site brewers, cideries, and
fruit wines.
I just want to make it be clear, you're not suggesting that
those provisions shouldn't be extended, are you?
No, no. Apologies if my submission was unclear.
We support the proposals in the legislation to broaden
the legislation to producer tasting rooms.
However, we are asking that the definition of a winery cellar door is
not removed, and it is implemented instead in Section 48,
which currently has what's arguably a quasi-definition of a winery cellar
door
that's been used in the past, now that we have something that's more fit for
purpose.
All right. Thank you.
Right. I think we're there.
Thank you for your submission to both of you, and thank you for turning up in
person too, by the way. All right.
Thank you.
Thank you.
We're going to Mr. Mandeep Singh.
Mr. Singh, I think you're coming on.
Am I doing good? Okay. Can you hear me?
Hello. We can certainly hear you. Can't see you.
Now, we've only got five minutes, Mr.
Singh, so we haven't got much time.
Okay.
Hello, kia ora, members of the committee.
My name is Mandeep Singh, and I live in South Waikato and have worked in
hospitality for over 14 years. Thank you for the opportunity to speak
today. I support the intent of this bill, particularly
the changes that improve fairness and transparency
in the licensing process.
The perspective I bring today comes from ground-level
experience. It does not come from a textbook, research paper, or
policy document. It comes from over 14 years of
working in hospitality, managing licensed premises, and dealing with these
issues in real time with real people and
with real communities. Over those years, I have spent countless
hours monitoring intoxication, looking after patrons,
supporting staff, arranging safe transport home, and doing my
best to provide a safe and welcoming environment for my
community.One thing I can honestly say is that I have
never worked alongside an operator who wanted harm in their
community.
I have never met
a venue owner, publican, or duty manager who wanted to see drink and
driving violence or alcohol-related harm.
One thing I have observed from my practical experience is that, at
times, the relationship between operators, duty managers, and
authorities can become adversarial when we would
likely achieve more through partnership.
I believe operators, regulators, health agencies, and
communities all ultimately want the same thing: safer
communities and less harm. When people are working towards the
same goal, communication, education, collaboration can
often achieve more than simply working against each other.
Recent data shows alcohol consumption and hazardous drinking have been
declining, yet alcohol-related harm remains a
challenge. I fully acknowledge that the harm exists.
Another condition,
and that it must be addressed. However, if harm remains despite
years of increased regulation, I think it is fair to ask whether we should
continue exploring different approaches alongside regulation.
Perhaps the answer is not always another rule, another condition,
or another compliance requirement.
Perhaps greater partnership between regulators, health agencies,
communities, and responsible operators could help achieve
far even better outcomes. Licensed venues
are often where people come to unwind after work, celebrate
milestones, catch up with family and friends, support local clubs.
Okay. So Mr.
Singh, can I ask you a question?
No, sure thing.
Because they're much more valuable.
Because everyone's read your submission, of course.
You made the comment and recommendations, license renewals must be decided within
a reasonable and fixed timeframe.
You said you've been operating for 14 years.
Can you give us your experience around getting
your renewals? First of all, how often do you have to do it?
What's the general license period?
Every.
And what's been your experience in that process?
Every three years, and the recent experience I have had
is
we applied for our renewal at one of the
venues in 2024, May,
and we
have got the license renewal after a hearing in
2026. That is over two years of
waiting under duress before-
And under the rules, during that period, if you had a
three-year license-
Yes
... do you technically become unlicensed?
Is that-
No, you can keep operating, but there's the uncertainty,
the duress.
Yeah.
You feel-
And so-
... waiting. Why should you be waiting for two years?
And tell me, you got taken, you actually had to go to a tribunal,
whatever what you said there.
Yes, hearing. Yes.
But did you
have a background where you'd had
non-compliance, or was there extenuating reasons why you

Yes, of course. There were a couple of incidents that needed to be addressed

Yes, there were incidents that could've been addressed and

to go on a hearing for those particular incidents that
happened before that time. They happened in 2022.
Right.
But they are discussed in 2025 or 2026.
Yes.
Yeah. Okay, cool. Right. Any other questions from our members?
I don't think so. Hey, thank you for joining us this afternoon.
You're welcome.
Really appreciate-
No problem

Bye.

please feel free.
Kia ora.

Five minutes.
Kia ora.
Yeah, no. I oppose this legislation, and I look at it
from a point of neuron disabilities.
Somebody that has got a host of neuron
disabilities. Now, I should say that my
mum never drunk throughout her life.
And my dad was very minimal. It'd be one pint, if anything
more.
I went from being 13-year-old to being sort of what you would
describe as being Asperger's, very
inverting, very shy, and so forth.
Until having one can of beer,
by the appointment of Her Majesty, it said on the can.
One can of Carlsberg Special Brew.
And for the next 10 years, I was a raving alcoholic.
It affected me in
multiple ways, through mental health, depression, and attempted suicide.
And that was after being put on antidepressants and having a
glass of wine, and that. I don't think the
problem is just that we look at mothers that drink.
I think it's we've formed communities now that it
is built into the person and so
forth. I stopped for over six years,
and I knew after one more drink, I'd start again. And I do.
I drink on my own because it's a lot safer
for everybodyAt 40 year old, I was on a
drug and alcohol review with Doug Sellman, who was the leading
authority on drug and alcohol at the time, and it was suggested
then that you never fully mentally develop from the age you started
drinking. And I would say that's very quite
accurate for myself. That at times, in the right
circumstances, I've got a mental health of 13-year-old child.
Which, yeah, being a 50-year-old man with mental health and mixed martial
arts isn't always a good perception.
I think when alcohol does sort of affect young people in the way and
so forth on the development of the brain, we have to
look at this way, and possibly it is to actually look at
legislation that looks at raising the age of drinking alcohol.
I understand that would be
not approved by yourselves and listening to last couple of

really about the care of the people that vote you into the elected
positions.
So yeah, I don't think I could get a trailer and go to any off
license and fill my van and my trailer with as much alcohol as I
wanted,
and that's the problem. Thank you.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. Hi Craig. Craig, you've-

Yeah

That has made me understand why you've

So thank you, and bless you.
Thank you.
All right. Hey, very nice to talk with you again,
Craig, and thank you for your contribution this afternoon. Okay?
All right.
Thank you. Bye-bye.

Yes, I am.
I
understand you'd like to be called Beer, so hopefully that's okay.

Okay. Well, whatever.

Hey, we've got five minutes with you, Beer.
Looking forward to hearing what you've got to say.
Well, Na ma ngaaki tona ki a koutou katoa.
E tene ahi hahi. Warm greetings to everybody this afternoon.
I just want to start by
noting, of course, that holding an alcohol license in New Zealand is a privilege
and not a right. I don't support any of the proposed changes to the
act.
And in particular, I don't support any restrictions on distance in
respect of objectors, and that's what I'm going to very briefly speak to
today. In particular, two things: where people live,
and then where their whanau live and their cultural
connections are. And to do this, I want to first draw on several
recent cases where we have had community objecting
to liquor licenses
here in Kerikeri, or applications here in Kerikeri and Waipapa.
Waipapa's a small neighboring settlement
outside of Kerikeri, which is largely a commercial and industrial
center on State Highway 10, with just a couple of residential
streets.
In this example,
all from the past few months, have involved two local major
bottle shops, a major supermarket, and our famous
Packhouse markets. Now, Kerikeri, I'm presuming that some
of you may have been here. It's notable that although our
population's around 8,000, it has 49 licensed premises.
That's 49. That's made up of 30 on licenses,
13 off licenses, and six clubs. And if you're doing the sums,
that's one liquor license for every 163 residents.
Now, the customers for these businesses live within a radius of
perhaps up to 50 kilometers, and sometimes much further away,
as Kerikeri's a hub for not only shopping,
but also for schools, childcare, medical, dental,
social, community, and other professional services.
And as a result, many people choose to purchase their alcohol whilst in town.
For example, people travel from as far west as Rawene in the
Hokianga. That's 70 kilometers. Coopers Beach up north here,
62 kilometers. And Russell to the east, a 39-kilometer
drive and a ferry ride too.
And unfortunately, all too often those customers open their alcohol on the
way home, not just increasing the risks of crashes while drink
driving, but as you'll observe if you're driving around most of the major
roads up here, the huge amounts of alcohol litter are usually
commencing within a few minutes of leaving these shopping settlements.
The same alcohol reaches our rural whanau, where serious harm such as
family violence and sexual abuse and assaults occur.
Simply, the Far North has the highest rates per capita of
family harm
in Aotearoa, substantially more than the average.
And of course, harm does not recognize a one or two kilometer boundary.
Our health and social service providers reflect a much higher
incidence of alcohol use during pregnancy, with rates quoted
from about one in 10 to almost half of our young people being adversely
affected in some way by that prenatal alcohol
exposure.
This does not recognize a one or two kilometer boundary.
I'd also draw your attention to those family members who are forced to seek
employment in other parts of Aotearoa New Zealand, or even Australia.
These people are often concerned about what happens for their family members still
at home, their well-being, and what sort of environment they're going to be
coming back to.
Limiting objections by distance will preclude these people from
ensuring their families are not subject unduly to
alcohol harm. They should have the opportunity to share their
concerns with the DLC, and it's over to the DLC to assess
the relevancy of such objections.
I think we're all pretty moved this morning by the testimony of the Sims family
regarding the tragic circumstances of their son's preventable
death.I don't support any restrictions on distance in
terms of objectors. And lastly, you will have read in my submission that
if you are limiting objections by distance in some respect,
then you'd also, I think, need to be considering the composition of the DLCs who
are charged with making those decisions about our local
communities. I've offered an example in my submission there, that
relates to professional members who sit on as many as 13
different DLCs throughout the country.
And in supporting our community's objections to the DLCs,
I've sort of realized that the unique factors which impact
our largely rural communities are not understood by these
professional contractors. So, if you do choose to recommend
any sort of
restrictions on distance for objectors, I ask that you make recommendations
imposing the same conditions on DLCs, ensuring that they are solely
comprised of members who live in the said limited area.
And so, in conclusion, I just want to ask that every decision that you make
regarding these proposed changes, that you consider each
proposal and the testimony you have heard and read against the object
of the act.
That's really important because that provides very specific aspiration
for reducing the pervasive direct and indirect harm
from alcohol in Aotearoa, New Zealand.
And I'm going to point out that even though the
parliamentary website says it, it is not an act which seeks to
improve economic growth.
Okay. All right. We're running out of time there.
We've got a question from Camilla.
Thank you. Thanks for your submission and for keeping

The question I had is just to kind of draw out your last comment a little bit more.
Do you think that there are aspects of this bill which are
inconsistent with the objectives of the primary act?
I think all of them are. I think that's why I've implored you to go that each time
you want to consider
the object to the act, because my experience, and I've worked with the old law
and the new laws, is that many of the people involved in the licensing
system still have a frame of mind that considers and
works within the context of the old law and not the new law.
Thank you.

people. You've worked in the Far North.
You talked about all those different off licenses, et cetera.
What was stopping the council from actually just declining them
or putting-
I'm sorry, I've not quite understand your question.
The-
What was stopping the Far North Council from just stopping the
whole proliferation of off licenses in Kerikeri?
Why couldn't they just do that?
Well, they need to have a local alcohol policy in place.
I was a Far North District Councillor.
And why didn't they do that?
Well, I was a Far North District Councillor when that went before the council,
and the majority of councillors, five to four, voted out
the LAP,
because of the fact that the industry itself were objecting and
said that they would take it
further, and that would basically bankrupt the council.
So we're a small council up here, and had already been 120,000.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah. All right. Hey, thanks for your time today.
It's been very helpful having firsthand experience.
Thank you.
Right. Now, we're going to the Rotorua Youth Council.
Hopefully, they've accepted. Are you there
with us?
Hello.
Yeah, you are. Fantastic. Right. Now, we've got 10 minutes with you.
Looking forward to hearing from all four of you.
The floor is yours, as they say.
Are you there?
Think we've lost them. Anna, have we lost
them?
Have we lost them?
Coming through.
Coming through or coming in again, Chair.

Are you there from the Rotorua Youth Council?
Yes.
Yes.
Oh, good. Right. Now, let's get started. We've got 10 minutes.
Our names are Atahua Jean, Ayush, Sarah, and Sakura,
and we are representing Rotorua Youth Council.
We're here today to speak on the Sales of Alcohol
Amendment Bill, because alcohol harm isn't just a policy debate to us,
it's a lived reality that negatively impacts our wider community and
deeply affects our own whanau. While this bill is framed around
cutting red tape businesses, we're here to talk about what happens when you
loosen the regulations, and who pays the price.
Our main focus points is increasing the availability of alcohol
fails to protect our most vulnerable, starting with our rangatahi.
Our first concern of how this bill further, excuse
me, further normalizes the regular exposure of alcohol to young children outside
the home. To give you a context of what this looks like on the ground,
I want to share the snapshot of my friend's whanau with you.
I have friends who have nieces and nephews who are just still babies.
I know their mother's currently on a hard journey recovering as an alcoholic.
Every single day she fights to stay sober for her kids, and we stand right
beside her through it. But the moment we step outside of her front door,
that fight becomes twice as hard. When our younger generation
see alcohol constantly present, and under this bill,
potentially expand into even more everyday spaces
like local barbers, hairdressers, or club events,
it normalizes the very thing that broken-- excuse me, it
normalizes the very thing that nearly broke their families apart.
